perturbation.original_text: For a period of three (3) years from the date of disclosure, Recipient agrees to use the same care and discretion to avoid disclosure, publication, or dissemination of Discloser's Confidential Information as it uses with its own similar information that it does not wish to disclose publish, or disseminate, but in no event shall Recipient use less than reasonable care to protect Discloser's Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: For a period of one (1) year from the date of disclosure, Recipient agrees to use the same care and discretion to avoid disclosure, publication, or dissemination of Discloser's Confidential Information as it uses with its own similar information that it does not wish to disclose publish, or disseminate, but in no event shall Recipient use less than reasonable care to protect Discloser's Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, which Utah law provides broader protection for.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under the Utah Uniform Trade Secrets Act, misappropriation can occur for as long as the trade secret retains its value and secrecy. Limiting the confidentiality period to one year may not adequately protect trade secrets, especially if the information retains its value and competitive advantage for a longer period.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Utah Code § 13-24-2 (2023) - Definitions. :: 2023 Utah Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Utah Code 2023 Utah Code (here) 2022 Utah Code View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 Utah Code Title 13 - Commerce and Trade Chapter 24 - Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 2 - Definitions. Universal Citation: UT Code § 13-24-2 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 13-24-2. Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret" me
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the confidentiality period from three years to one year. The Law Explanation states that under the Utah Uniform Trade Secrets Act, misappropriation can occur for as long as the trade secret retains its value and secrecy. The Perturbation Explanation also suggests that a one-year period may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under Utah law. Scraped Law Snippet 2, Utah Code § 13-24-2, defines 'misappropriation' and indicates that it involves the 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent.' By shortening the confidentiality period, the modified text increases the risk of misappropriation as defined by Utah law, especially if the trade secret's value extends beyond one year, thus creating a legal contradiction and potentially violating the Utah Uniform Trade Secrets Act.

perturbation.original_text: Upon termination of this Agreement, or earlier upon Discloser's request, Recipient shall promptly return or destroy all documents and tangible items in its possession which contain any part of the Confidential Information of Discloser.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of this Agreement, or earlier upon Discloser's request, Recipient shall retain all documents and tangible items in its possession which contain any part of the Confidential Information of Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring the recipient to retain confidential information after termination contradicts the general principle of returning or destroying such information, potentially violating trade secret laws if misused.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 13-24-3 prohibits the misappropriation of trade secrets. Retaining and potentially using confidential information after the agreement's termination, rather than returning or destroying it, directly contradicts the intent to protect trade secrets as outlined in the law and increases the risk of misappropriation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Utah Code § 13-24-3 (2023) - Injunctive relief. :: 2023 Utah Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Utah Code 2023 Utah Code (here) 2022 Utah Code View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 Utah Code Title 13 - Commerce and Trade Chapter 24 - Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 3 - Injunctive relief. Universal Citation: UT Code § 13-24-3 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 13-24-3. Injunctive relief. (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. Enacted by Chapter 60, 1989 General Session Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Utah may have more current or accurate information. perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the intent of trade secret law, specifically Utah Code § 13-24-3, which aims to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. The original text mandates the return or destruction of confidential information upon termination, aligning with the protection of trade secrets. However, the changed text requires the recipient to *retain* this information, increasing the risk of misappropriation, which the law seeks to prevent. The law snippet states that "actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined," clearly indicating the legal system's intention to prevent the misuse of trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: In any legal proceeding arising out of this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to an award of its costs and reasonable attorneys' fees.
perturbation.changed_text: In any legal proceeding arising out of this Agreement, each party shall bear its own costs and attorneys' fees.
perturbation.explanation: Eliminating the prevailing party's entitlement to attorney's fees may conflict with Utah law in specific cases, particularly those involving trade secret misappropriation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under the Utah Uniform Trade Secrets Act, § 13-24-4(1), a court may award reasonable attorney's fees to the prevailing party if (a) a claim of misappropriation is made in bad faith, (b) a motion to terminate an injunction is made or resisted in bad faith, or (c) willful and malicious misappropriation exists. Changing the contract to state that each party shall bear its own costs and attorneys' fees contradicts the court's power to award attorney's fees to a prevailing party in the scenarios listed under § 13-24-4(1).
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Utah Code § 13-24-4 (2023) - Damages. :: 2023 Utah Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Utah Code 2023 Utah Code (here) 2022 Utah Code View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 Utah Code Title 13 - Commerce and Trade Chapter 24 - Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 4 - Damages. Universal Citation: UT Code § 13-24-4 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 13-24-4. Damages. (1) Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (2) If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under Subsection (1). Enacted by Chapter 60, 1989 General Session Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Utah may have more current or accurate information. perturbation.justification: The changed text, which stipulates that each party bears its own costs and attorneys' fees in any legal proceeding, directly contradicts the Utah Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Specifically, the Law Explanation highlights § 13-24-4(1), which grants the court the power to award reasonable attorney's fees to the prevailing party under certain conditions such as bad faith claims or willful misappropriation. Scraped Law Snippet 2, citing Utah Code § 13-24-4 (2023), focuses on damages related to trade secret misappropriation but further supports the legal context within which attorney's fees can be awarded by the court. By removing the possibility of awarding attorney's fees to the prevailing party, the modified contract undermines the court's statutory authority as granted under Utah law, thereby creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of Utah (without regard to conflicts of laws provisions).
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of Delaware (without regard to conflicts of laws provisions).
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware could conflict with Utah's interest in protecting trade secrets originating and used primarily within Utah.
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can generally choose governing law, a court may refuse to enforce a choice-of-law provision if it violates a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest in the litigation. If the trade secrets are created and primarily used in Utah, Utah has a strong interest in applying its own trade secret laws (Utah Uniform Trade Secrets Act) to protect those trade secrets. Delaware law may offer different levels of protection or remedies, potentially undermining Utah's policy.

perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces Utah law with Delaware law, potentially contradicting Utah's interest in protecting trade secrets originating and used primarily within Utah. The Utah Uniform Trade Secrets Act, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1 and Scraped Law Snippet 2, indicates Utah's intent to govern trade secret matters within its jurisdiction. By choosing Delaware law, the contract undermines Utah's ability to apply its own trade secret laws, particularly if Delaware law provides different or weaker protections.
